text:
Your working directory is a checkout of the real yet-another-react-lightbox repository at the commit where the bug below reproduces. Find and fix the bug in the library source.

Bug: this library renders a photo/video lightbox with an optional download plugin that lets the user save the currently displayed image. When the image lives on a different origin than the page, the plugin can't just point a plain anchor tag at it (cross-origin downloads via a plain `<a download>` don't reliably work), so it fetches the image itself over XHR and then hands the resulting Blob, together with an optional caller-supplied filename, to a small local `saveAs` helper that triggers the actual save. If the caller didn't configure an explicit filename for the slide/download, the saved file should still get a meaningful name: the HTTP response for the image may include a `Content-Disposition` header naming the file server-side (e.g. `attachment; filename="photo.jpg"`, or the RFC 5987 extended form `filename*=UTF-8''caf%C3%A9.jpg` for non-ASCII names), and that name should be used as a fallback. Right now, nothing in the cross-origin download path ever looks at this response header at all, so whenever no explicit filename is configured, the file always saves under a generic name the browser derives from the blob URL instead of the name the server intended.

Expected behavior once fixed, spelled out precisely since it's pure header-parsing logic:
- A missing/null header, an empty header, or a header with no `filename`/`filename*` parameter at all (e.g. just `inline`) means there's no server-provided name to fall back to.
- A plain `filename` parameter may be unquoted (`filename=photo.jpg`) or double-quoted (`filename="photo.jpg"`); use the value as-is with surrounding whitespace trimmed and surrounding quotes stripped. Extra whitespace around the `=` or after the value before the next `;` (e.g. `filename = photo.jpg ; size=1024`) should not break parsing.
- An empty quoted value (`filename=""`) counts as no filename.
- Per RFC 5987/6266, when both an extended `filename*=UTF-8''<percent-encoded>` parameter and a plain `filename` parameter are present, the extended (percent-decoded) one takes precedence, e.g. `filename*=UTF-8''na%C3%AFve.jpg` should decode to `naïve.jpg`.
- If the `filename*` value has malformed percent-encoding, or decodes to an empty/whitespace-only string, parsing must not throw -- it should fall back to the plain `filename` parameter instead, if one is present.

Environment notes: dependencies are already installed (Node, npm). You can scope the existing Vitest suite to whatever file you're iterating on with `npx vitest run <path-to-file>`. Fix the library source; do not just add workarounds in test files.

text: I’ll trace the download plugin’s cross-origin request and existing test conventions, then implement header parsing at the library boundary and run the focused Vitest coverage plus relevant broader checks.

text: The cross-origin flow is concentrated in `src/plugins/download/FileSaver.ts`: the XHR currently passes only the caller’s `name` into `saveAs`, so the response metadata is discarded. I’ll add a small parser there, give an explicit name priority, and use a valid `filename*` or `filename` value only when no usable explicit name exists.

The source now reads `Content-Disposition` only after the XHR completes and preserves the precedence rule: caller filename, then valid extended server filename, then plain server filename. I’m tightening edge handling and checking the repository’s lint/type behavior before running the focused suite.

I’m making the parser tolerant of semicolons inside quoted values as well, while keeping whitespace and quote handling aligned with the specified parameter forms.

text: The parser change is in place and the focused test, formatting/lint, and JavaScript build checks are running now. The build is useful here because it validates the source through the project’s actual Rollup/TypeScript pipeline, not just Vitest’s transform.

All runnable checks passed: the focused Download suite (4 tests), ESLint, Prettier, and the JavaScript build. The standalone `tsc --noEmit` check remains unavailable because of the repository’s pre-existing TypeScript configuration deprecation, so I’m doing a final diff/status audit to ensure only the intended source change remains.

input.command: /bin/bash -lc 'git status --short && git diff --stat && git diff -- src/plugins/download/FileSaver.ts'

text:
Fixed cross-origin downloads to use `Content-Disposition` filenames.

- Supports `filename` and RFC 5987 `filename*`
- Extended UTF-8 names take precedence
- Malformed/empty extended values fall back safely
- Explicit caller filenames remain authoritative

Validation passed: focused Vitest suite, ESLint, Prettier, and Rollup build. `tsc --noEmit` remains blocked by the repository’s existing TypeScript 6 `moduleResolution=node10` deprecation.
